questions.question: Define custom exceptions in Java and explain their primary purpose.
keyPoints: Custom exceptions are user-defined exception classes that extend the Exception class.
Their primary purpose is to handle application-specific error conditions that are not adequately represented by Java's built-in exceptions.

questions.question: Explain the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java.
keyPoints: Checked exceptions extend the Exception class and are checked at compile-time.
Unchecked exceptions extend the RuntimeException class and are not checked at compile-time.
Checked exceptions are used for recoverable conditions, while unchecked exceptions are used for programming errors or unrecoverable conditions.

questions.question: Compare and contrast custom exceptions with Java's built-in exceptions.
keyPoints: Custom exceptions provide more specific and meaningful error messages than Java's built-in exceptions.
Custom exceptions are used to represent specific error scenarios in an application, while Java's built-in exceptions are used for general error scenarios.
Custom exceptions can be used to improve code readability and maintainability by providing clear and concise error messages.

questions.question: Design a custom exception hierarchy for a banking application and justify your design choices.
keyPoints: The base BankingException should be a checked exception (extends Exception) as most banking errors are recoverable conditions.
The InsufficientFundsException and InvalidAccountException should be subclasses of BankingException and include instance variables to provide detailed error context.
The design should take into account the use of checked vs. unchecked exceptions and provide clear and concise error messages.

questions.question: Explain the benefits of using custom exceptions in Java and provide an example of how they can be used to improve code readability and maintainability.
keyPoints: Custom exceptions provide more specific and meaningful error messages than Java's built-in exceptions.
Custom exceptions can be used to improve code readability and maintainability by providing clear and concise error messages.
Custom exceptions can be used to represent specific error scenarios in an application, making it easier to diagnose and fix problems.
